Define \(\psi_{k,l}=\min\{\phi_{k},\cdots, \phi_{k+l}\}\text{.}\) Then \(\psi_{k,l}\in L^{1}[a, b]\) and \(\psi_{k,l}(x)\ge \psi_{k,l+1}(x)\) \(a.e.\text{.}\) Then Theorem 2.17 implies that \(\Phi_{k}=\lim_{l\to\infty} \psi_{k,l} \in L^{1}[a, b]\) with \(\int_{a}^{b} \Phi_{k} = \lim_{l\to\infty}\int_{a}^{b} \psi_{k,l}\text{.}\) Since for any \(l > l' \ge k\text{,}\) \(\psi_{k,l} \le \phi_{l'}\text{,}\) it follows that \(\int_{a}^{b} \Phi_{k} \le \int_{a}^{b} \phi_{l'}\text{,}\) therefore, \(\int_{a}^{b} \Phi_{k} \le \inf\{ \int_{a}^{b} \phi_{l'}: l'\ge k\}\text{.}\)
Now \(\Phi_{k} \le \Phi_{k+1} \) and \(\int_{a}^{b} \Phi_{k} \) is bounded under our assumption. Then Theorem 2.17 implies \(\lim_{k\to\infty} \Phi_{k} \in L^{1}[a, b]\) and
\begin{equation*} \int_{a}^{b} \liminf_{k\to \infty} \phi_{k}(x) = \int_{a}^{b} \lim_{k\to\infty} \Phi_{k} = \lim_{k\to\infty} \int_{a}^{b} \Phi_{k} \le \lim_{k\to\infty} \inf\{ \int_{a}^{b} \phi_{l'}: l'\ge k\}, \end{equation*}
which equals \(\liminf_{k\to \infty} \int_{a}^{b} \phi_{k}(x)\text{.}\)
